15: veevah asit beincha uvein haisha uvein zaracha uvein zarah tshuv yeshuphenu rosh veata teshuphenu akev: 'I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.' The protoevangelium (first gospel). Key philological issues: (1) The singular zarah (offspring) combined with the masculine singular pronoun hu (he) in the LXX translated as masculine singular autos — a masculine seed of the woman — though the Hebrew zera is collectively singular and the pronoun could be collective. Jewish midrash and Church Fathers both recognized the Messianic potential; (2) shuph (bruise/strike): used for both the heel-strike and the head-strike with the same verb — the same action, different targets, different outcomes. The serpent's strike is temporary (heel); the seed's strike is fatal (head).
1:
עָרוּם (ʿārûm) — "shrewd/crafty." The same adjective used positively in Proverbs (12:16, 23; 13:16; 14:8, 15, 18; 22:3; 27:12) for prudent wisdom — here it is the craft that circumvents rather than serves wisdom. The deliberate wordplay with 2:25 (ʿărûmmîm, naked) is a literary signal: the shrewdness of the serpent exploits the openness/nakedness of the human pair.

אַף כִּי-אָמַר אֱלֹהִים — "Did God really say...?" The serpent's opening is not a direct denial but a question that introduces doubt about the scope of the command. Note: the serpent uses ʾĕlōhîm (not YHWH ʾĕlōhîm of ch 2-3), subtly distancing the covenant name. The question misrepresents the command — God had permitted all trees; the serpent asks whether God had prohibited all trees.
2: The woman correctly identifies the scope of the permission ("we may eat from the trees in the garden") but her response already engages the serpent's framing. She corrects the misrepresentation but will in v. 3 add to the prohibition — "you must not touch it" — a slight expansion of the original command (2:17 mentions only eating, not touching). Whether this is her memory, her interpretation, or her own addition, it begins the process of misrepresenting the divine word.
3: לֹא תֹאכְלוּ מִמֶּנּוּ וְלֹא תִגְּעוּ בּוֹ — "you must not eat from it, and you must not touch it." The addition of "touch" (nāgaʿ) is the woman's addition to the original command (2:17). The prohibition of touching as well as eating represents either a traditional fence around the law (expanding the prohibition to prevent accidental violation), a memory error, or a deliberate elaboration. In any case, the text does not present it as God's word — and the serpent will shortly deny the truth of the word as the woman has stated it.
4: לֹא-מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן (lōʾ-môt tĕmutûn) — "you will not certainly die." The serpent uses the same emphatic construction God used in 2:17 (môt tāmût) but negated. It is a direct contradiction of divine speech. The three-part structure of the temptation follows: (1) question/doubt (v. 1), (2) direct denial (v. 4), (3) alternative appeal to self-interest (v. 5).
5: כֵּאלֹהִים יֹדְעֵי טוֹב וָרָע (kēʾlōhîm yōḏĕʿê ṭôb wārāʿ) — "like God [or: like gods], knowing good and evil." ʾĔlōhîm is grammatically ambiguous — it could be "like God" (singular referent) or "like the divine beings/gods" (plural). The serpent's claim is that eating will elevate the humans to divine-level autonomous knowledge. The irony: after the Fall, God himself says "the man has become like one of us, knowing good and evil" (3:22) — the serpent's claim is partially confirmed but the cost and mode were not disclosed.
6:
כִּי טוֹב הָעֵץ לְמַאֲכָל וְכִי תַאֲוָה-הוּא לָעֵינַיִם — the threefold evaluation mirrors but corrupts the creation evaluations. The woman sees (1) good for food (ṭôb lĕmaʾăkāl), (2) desirable to the eye (taʾăwāh hûʾ lāʿênāyim), (3) desirable for gaining wisdom (neḥmāḏ hāʿēṣ lĕhaśkîl). The three appeals correspond to what 1 John 2:16 calls "the desire of the flesh, the desire of the eyes, and the pride of life." Taʾăwāh (desire, craving) is the same word used of the Israelites' craving in the wilderness (Num 11:4; Ps 106:14).

וַתִּתֵּן גַּם-לְאִישָׁהּ עִמָּהּ וַיֹּאכַל — "and she also gave some to her husband who was with her (ʿimmāh), and he ate." The final phrase is shocking in its brevity: he was with her. Adam was present; he does not argue, question, or resist. His sin is not deception but passive acquiescence — which Paul distinguishes (1 Tim 2:14: "Adam was not deceived, but the woman was deceived and became a transgressor").
7:
וַתִּפָּקַחְנָה עֵינֵי שְׁנֵיהֶם (wattippāqaḥnāh ʿênê šĕnêhem) — "and the eyes of both of them were opened." The verb pāqaḥ (to open eyes, to have sight restored) is used of Hagar's eyes being opened to see a well (21:19), of Elisha's prayer that Gehazi's eyes be opened (2 Kgs 6:17), of the blind receiving sight. The irony is that the eyes opened to knowledge that shamed rather than illuminated — they see themselves for the first time as exposed and deficient.

וַיֵּדְעוּ כִּי עֵירֻמִּם הֵם (wayyēdĕʿû kî ʿêrummim hēm) — "and they knew that they were naked." The first thing the newly awakened moral knowledge produces is not wisdom but shame. ʿEirummim is a variant spelling of 2:25's ʿărûmmîm — the connection is deliberate. They were naked before and unashamed; now they are naked and ashamed. The knowledge they gained is first of their own inadequacy before God.
8: אֶת-קוֹל יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּגָּן לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם — "the sound/voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day." Qôl is "voice" or "sound" — the humans hide from the approaching qôl of God, not necessarily a visible form. Lĕrûaḥ hayyôm — "in the cool of the day" — literally "in the wind/spirit of the day," the late afternoon breeze. The walking suggests a regular pattern: God's presence in the garden was not unusual. Hiding was.
9: אַיֶּכָּה (ʾayyekāh) — "Where are you?" The rhetorical question is not informational — God knows. It is an invitation to confess, to come out, to be found. The same form appears in Lam 1:1's "Alas!" (ʾêkāh) over desolated Jerusalem — a connection the rabbis made explicitly. God's "Where are you?" to Adam is echoed in the lament over fallen Israel. The question opens the judicial process that follows.
10: אֶת-קֹלְךָ שָׁמַעְתִּי בַּגָּן וָאִירָא — "I heard your voice in the garden and I was afraid." The first instance of fear in Genesis — and of humans fearing God. Before the Fall, God walked in the garden and the humans were unashamed; now the same divine presence produces fear. The disruption of the relationship is total: shame toward each other (v. 7), fear toward God (v. 10). Fear of God will later become a virtue in Israel (the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, Prov 9:10) — but this first fear is guilt-driven hiding, not reverent awe.
11: God's interrogation moves from location ("Where are you?") to action ("Who told you... Have you eaten...?"). The questions are forensic — establishing facts in the manner of a judicial proceeding. The Hebrew word order in "Have you eaten from the tree from which I commanded you not to eat?" puts the tree first for emphasis: the tree was the prohibited object; eating from it is the charge.
12: הָאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר נָתַתָּה עִמָּדִי — "The woman whom you gave to be with me." Adam's response implicates both the woman and God: you gave points upward, she gave me points sideways. The man who identified the woman as "bone of my bones" (2:23) now distances himself from her with a relative clause. The relational solidarity of 2:23-24 is instantly fractured. The syntax is revealing: "the woman — whom you gave — she gave me" — the blame-chain is grammatically stacked.
13: הַנָּחָשׁ הִשִּׁיאַנִי (hannāḥāš hiššîʾanî) — "the serpent deceived me." Nāšāʾ (Hiphil) means to lead astray, deceive. The woman does not deny the act but names the deceiver. Paul distinguishes Eve's deception from Adam's choice (2 Cor 11:3; 1 Tim 2:14) — both sinned, but by different mechanisms. The woman's response is more honest than the man's (she confesses "and I ate" directly); the man's response contained no explicit confession of eating.
14: כִּי עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת — "because you did this." The serpent's curse is unconditional — no interrogation, no opportunity to respond. The serpent's curse has two parts: (1) lowest among animals, crawling and eating dust; (2) enmity with the woman. The crawling on its belly is a cursed abasement — whatever the serpent's original form, it is now permanently lowered. The dust-eating is likely a metaphor for utter defeat (Ps 72:9; Isa 49:23; Mic 7:17) rather than literal dietary prescription.
16:
הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה עִצְּבוֹנֵךְ וְהֵרֹנֵךְ — "I will greatly increase your pain in childbearing." The emphatic infinitive absolute construction (harbāh ʾarbbeh, doubling the verbal root) stresses the intensification. ʿIṣṣābôn (pain/toil) is the same word used of the man's agricultural toil in v. 17 — both curses are expressed in the same vocabulary, suggesting a structural parallel between the woman's domain (childbearing) and the man's (ground-working), both now marked by painful labor.

וְאֶל-אִישֵׁךְ תְּשׁוּקָתֵךְ וְהוּא יִמְשָׁל-בָּךְ — "your desire (tĕšûqāh) will be for your husband, and he will rule (māšal) over you." Tĕšûqāh (desire/longing) appears three times in the OT: here, in Song of Songs 7:10 (the husband's desire for the wife — reciprocal), and Gen 4:7 (sin's desire to control Cain — context is mastery/control). The word's connotation in 4:7 has led some to interpret the woman's desire here as a desire to control or dominate, with the man's ruling as the counteracting consequence. Others read it as a positive longing rendered unequal by the man's rule. Either way, the ordered partnership of 2:18-24 is now marked by tension and asymmetry — a description of the fallen state, not a prescription for the ideal order.
17: אֲרוּרָה הָאֲדָמָה בַּעֲבוּרֶךָ (ʾărûrāh hāʾăḏāmāh baʿăḇûrekā) — "cursed is the ground because of you." Note: the man is not cursed directly (as the serpent was, v. 14) but the ʾăḏāmāh (ground) is cursed because of him. The wordplay between ʾāḏām (man) and ʾăḏāmāh (ground) — established at 2:7 — now carries its full weight: the man is locked in a cursed relationship with the very source from which he was formed. ʿIṣṣābôn (toil/pain) — the same word as the woman's pain in v. 16: both curses produce ʿiṣṣābôn, labor transformed into painful striving.
18: קוֹץ וְדַרְדַּר (qôṣ wĕdardar) — "thorns and thistles." The earth produces what opposes cultivation rather than facilitating it. The same pairing appears in Hos 10:8 as an image of divine judgment. The thorn/thistle combination signals that the ground is no longer a willing partner in the man's vocation — it resists, frustrates, and competes with the cultivated crop.
19: בְּזֵעַת אַפֶּיךָ תֹּאכַל לֶחֶם (bĕzēʿat ʾappêkā tōʾkal leḥem) — "by the sweat of your face you will eat bread." The preposition phrase establishes the new mode: by painful exertion, not the easy abundance of Eden. עַד שׁוּבְךָ אֶל-הָאֲדָמָה כִּי מִמֶּנָּה לֻקָּחְתָּ — "until you return to the ground, for from it you were taken." The Pual perfect luqqāḥtā (you were taken) connects back to 2:7 and 2:23 — the same verb describes the rib taken from Adam to make Eve. Now the man is taken from the ground and will be returned to it. כִּי-עָפָר אַתָּה וְאֶל-עָפָר תָּשׁוּב — "for dust you are, and to dust you shall return." The inclusio with 2:7 (ʿāpār min-hāʾăḏāmāh) frames the human story: dust-sourced, dust-destined.
20: חַוָּה (ḥawwāh) — "Eve." The name is connected to ḥay (living) — she is "the mother of all living" (ʾēm kol-ḥāy). The naming of his wife after the curse is itself a statement of faith: in the moment of the death-sentence, the man names the woman "Life-giver." The act anticipates the zeraʿ promise of v. 15 — through the woman, life will continue and the promised offspring will come.
21: כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר (kotnôt ʿôr) — "garments of skin." God himself clothes the human pair — the first act of divine provision after judgment. The skin garments require the death of an animal; whether this implies sacrifice is implied but not stated. The contrast with the fig-leaf coverings (ḥăgorāh, 3:7) is significant: human covering is insufficient and temporary; divine covering is permanent and adequate. The theological tradition reads this as the first atonement-picture.
22:
הֵן הָאָדָם הָיָה כְּאַחַד מִמֶּנּוּ לָדַעַת טוֹב וָרָע — "Behold, the man has become like one of us, knowing good and evil." The serpent's promise (3:5) was not entirely false — the knowledge was gained. But what the serpent concealed was the cost and the mode: knowledge through disobedience, not through maturation under God's guidance. The divine "us" recurs (as in 1:26) — still unexplained in the text itself.

וְעַתָּה פֶּן-יִשְׁלַח יָדוֹ — "and now, lest he reach out his hand." The concern is not that immortality would be too good for the fallen human but that immortality in the condition of moral ruin would be unremitting misery — a fallen being locked into endless life. The expulsion from Eden is thus an act of mercy as well as judgment: the way to the tree of life is blocked until the condition that makes the tree deadly can be reversed.
23: וַיְשַׁלְּחֵהוּ יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִגַּן-עֵדֶן (wayĕšallĕḥēhû YHWH ʾĕlōhîm miggan-ʿēḏen) — "and the LORD God sent him out from the Garden of Eden." Šāllaḥ (Piel, intensive) means to send out forcefully — used of divorce (Deut 24:1-3) and of expulsion. The same verb will appear when Abraham sends out Hagar (21:14) and when the Egyptians expel Israel (Exod 12:33). The divine expulsion is an irrevocable change of status.
24:
אֶת-הַכְּרֻבִים (ʾet-hakkĕrubîm) — "the cherubim." The cherubim appear here for the first time — divine guardians who prevent unauthorized access to the holy. They will appear as the guardians of the ark of the covenant (Exod 25:18-22; 37:7-9), woven into the tabernacle curtains (Exod 26:1), carved into Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:23-29), and in Ezekiel's throne-chariot visions (Ezek 1:5-25; 10). They are the guardians of the divine throne and presence; here they guard the tree of life.

אֵת לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת (ʾêt lahaṭ haḥereḇ hammithappe|ket) — "the flaming/turning sword." Lahaṭ (flame) + the Hitpael participle of hāpak (to turn, overturn) — the sword turns in every direction, covering every angle of approach. The garden is sealed: the cherubim guard the eastern entrance, the sword covers all other approaches. The way back to Eden is completely blocked — until Revelation 22 shows the tree of life in the New Jerusalem, accessible again.
